description: How players get to act: worker placement, action selection, action points, programmed moves, and the timing structures around turns.

placement.description: Claiming actions by physically placing limited worker pieces on spaces.

focuses.worker_placement: Players take turns placing a limited supply of workers on action spaces to perform those actions (as in Agricola or Lords of Waterdeep).
focuses.blocked_spaces: An occupied action space is unavailable to later workers, making every placement both an action and a denial (as in Agricola).
focuses.worker_retrieval_timing: Workers stay out after placement, and choosing when to spend a turn recalling them is itself a strategic decision (as in Tzolk'in or The Manhattan Project).
focuses.escalating_placement_cost: Placing costs progressively more the later or more crowded the placement is (rising corn costs for extra workers in Tzolk'in).
focuses.worker_types_upgrades: Workers come in different types or strengths with different placement rights, and can be upgraded during play (the grande worker in Viticulture).
focuses.worker_bumping: A placed worker can displace one already on the space, usually compensating or relocating the victim (knight displacement in Lancaster).
focuses.dice_workers: Rolled dice serve as the workers, with pip values constraining where they may be placed or how well they perform (as in Alien Frontiers or The Voyages of Marco Polo).
focuses.accumulating_action_spaces: Untaken spaces accumulate goods each round, growing more tempting until someone finally claims the pile (accumulation spaces in Agricola).
focuses.worker_feeding_upkeep: Workers must periodically be fed or paid, taxing expansion and punishing overextension (harvests in Agricola).
focuses.staged_placement_resolution: All workers are placed first and then spaces resolve in a fixed order, separating commitment from payoff (the road in Caylus).
focuses.player_built_action_spaces: Players construct new action spaces that all players may use, often paying the builder for the privilege (buildings in Caylus or Lords of Waterdeep).
focuses.placement_on_opponent_boards: Workers may be placed on components owned by opponents, benefiting placer and owner in different ways (placing in rivals' villages in Keyflower).
focuses.worker_pool_growth: Players can expand their worker supply during play, and timing that growth against its cost is a defining decision (family growth in Agricola).

selection.description: Choosing actions from menus, pools, roles, and wheels.

focuses.role_selection: Each player picks a role that grants a phase or action to everyone, with a privilege reserved for the chooser (as in Puerto Rico or Citadels).
focuses.action_drafting: Players pick actions from a shared limited pool that depletes as picks are made, so options vanish over the round (strategy cards in Twilight Imperium).
focuses.rondel_selection: Actions sit on a wheel and a player's token moves a limited number of steps around it, paying extra to reach farther actions (as in Imperial or Antike).
focuses.action_retrieval_mancala: Action markers are picked up and sown across a track mancala-style, with the landing spot determining the action taken (as in Trajan or Five Tribes).
focuses.follow_actions: When one player takes an action, others may follow with a weaker version of it, keeping everyone involved off-turn (as in Glory to Rome or Eminent Domain).
focuses.simultaneous_selection: All players secretly choose their action and reveal at once, with outcomes shaped by the mix chosen (as in Libertalia or Race for the Galaxy).
focuses.action_cards_hand: A hand of cards defines which actions are available, and playing a card both acts and depletes the hand until it is recovered (as in Concordia).
focuses.dice_action_pools: Dice are rolled to form the round's menu of available actions, with the results constraining what anyone can do (as in Grand Austria Hotel).
focuses.stale_action_bonuses: Actions left unchosen accumulate bonuses over time, sweetening neglected options until someone takes them (doubloons on roles in Puerto Rico, card strength in Ark Nova).
focuses.exhaust_and_refresh_actions: A player's own action options are spent when used and only return after a rest or refresh step, forcing pacing decisions (card play and rests in Gloomhaven).
focuses.asymmetric_action_menus: Each player or faction has a structurally different set of available actions, so the same turn works differently in every seat (as in Root).

points.description: Budgeting how much can be done per turn through points, slots, and limits.

focuses.action_point_allowance: Each turn grants a budget of action points to spend freely across a menu of costed actions (as in Tikal or Pandemic).
focuses.variable_action_counts: The number of actions a player gets per turn varies with faction, board state, or upgrades rather than being fixed (civil actions under different governments in Through the Ages).
focuses.exhaustion_limits: Pieces or cards exhaust when used and cannot act again until readied, limiting activations per round (tapping in Magic: The Gathering).
focuses.free_actions: Certain minor actions cost no points or slots and can be taken alongside normal actions (free object interactions in D&D 5e).
focuses.action_point_carryover: Unspent action points can be saved for future turns, letting players bank tempo (as in Torres).
focuses.split_action_ownership: Each player controls only a subset of the action types while all pieces are shared, forcing wordless coordination (as in Magic Maze).
focuses.structured_action_types: A turn provides distinct slots for different categories of action rather than interchangeable points (action, bonus action, and movement in D&D 5e; top and bottom row actions in Scythe).

programming.description: Committing to sequences of actions before they resolve.

focuses.simultaneous_programming: All players secretly plan a sequence of actions, then everyone's programs execute together (as in RoboRally).
focuses.movement_programming: Movement specifically is plotted in advance with cards or dials, and pieces execute the plotted course even when circumstances have changed (as in RoboRally or Colt Express).
focuses.queued_execution: Declared actions enter a queue and resolve later in a fixed order, so plans must survive everything that resolves before them (order execution in Wallenstein).
focuses.hidden_orders: Order tokens are placed face down on the board and revealed for resolution, concealing intent until the moment of commitment (as in A Game of Thrones: The Board Game).
focuses.program_disruption: Board effects or other players can shift, rotate, or damage a plotted program between planning and execution (robot pushing in RoboRally, tunnels in Colt Express).
focuses.command_line_upgrades: A player's program persists between rounds and is modified incrementally rather than rebuilt, becoming a growing machine (the command line in Mechs vs. Minions).
focuses.order_conflict_resolution: Simultaneously revealed orders interact by supporting, cutting, or cancelling one another, with a resolution procedure deciding outcomes (as in Diplomacy).
focuses.solo_automa_scripts: A scripted deck or flowchart drives a non-player opponent's actions, simulating an adversary through programmed priorities (the Automa in Wingspan or Scythe).

timing.description: When actions happen: turn order, interrupts, clocks, and round structure.

focuses.time_track_turns: Turn order follows a time track where the player furthest behind acts next, so cheap actions buy extra turns (as in Thebes, Patchwork, or Glen More).
focuses.interrupt_actions: Players can act outside their turn in response to triggers, inserting effects into others' actions (instants in Magic: The Gathering, opportunity attacks in D&D).
focuses.delayed_effects: Some actions resolve one or more turns after being taken, rewarding anticipation over reaction (slow powers in Spirit Island).
focuses.real_time_actions: Players act simultaneously against a clock rather than in turns, making speed itself a resource (as in Galaxy Trucker or Space Alert).
focuses.turn_order_selection: Players choose their position in the next round's turn order as an in-game decision with attached costs or perks (the wake-up track in Viticulture).
focuses.pass_and_out: Passing removes a player from the rest of the round, so staying in longer trades exposure for opportunity (as in Ra or 18xx stock rounds).
focuses.simultaneous_turns: All players take their turns at the same time with little or no interaction locking, compressing play time (roll-and-writes like Cartographers, the ages of 7 Wonders).
focuses.time_as_resource: Time itself is spent as a cost, with actions consuming time units that age workers or advance a personal clock (as in Village).
focuses.initiative_order: Acting order within a round is derived from rolled or computed initiative values rather than seating (initiative in D&D).
focuses.round_phase_structure: Each round follows a fixed sequence of named phases that gate which actions can occur when (the phase sequence in Twilight Imperium, work and harvest in Agricola).
